keyInsights.insight: The single highest-leverage signal that an agent is in the weeds is REPETITION WITHOUT STATE CHANGE: it re-edits the same file/region, re-runs the same failing command, or re-proposes a variant of an approach it already tried — and the error/test output is unchanged. This is detectable mechanically (fingerprint = tool name + target + diff of the error output), not just by vibes.
keyInsights.why: Repetition-without-progress is the observable surface of every deeper pathology (tunnel vision, sunk cost, local minima). AWS's measured experiment showed a travel agent looping 14 tool calls where 2 sufficed; framework-level deduplication and observability guides both converge on 'identical/near-identical call within a sliding window' as the primary trip-wire. It is the one signal you can detect without a judge model, so it should be the backbone of the guardrail.
keyInsights.evidence: AWS DebounceHook fingerprints 'tool name + exact inputs' and blocks a repeat within a 3-call window; Latitude recommends 'detect identical tool calls within a session (same arguments, repeated invocation)' and 'session step count outliers' as primary failure signals; the 14-to-2 (7x) reduction came purely from making terminal states unambiguous.

keyInsights.insight: Agents fixate because they are GREEDY single-trajectory committers with no backtrack primitive. Once an early action is bad, there is no mechanism in the default loop to reconsider it — the agent rationalizes forward instead of reverting. This is an architectural property, not a prompting deficiency, which is why 'try harder' prompts fail and why the fix must be a loop mechanism that forces branching/reset.
keyInsights.why: This reframes the whole design: you cannot 'prompt away' fixation because the loop itself has no reverse gear. The workflow must supply the reverse gear (revert-to-last-good-state, fresh session, enumerate-alternatives) as an explicit mechanism, because the model will not generate it spontaneously when already committed.
keyInsights.evidence: DARS (arXiv 2503.14269): 'Once an agent commits to a poor action early... it cannot escape that compromised trajectory because there is no mechanism to backtrack.' Re-sampling/branching at decision points measurably beats greedy baselines on SWE-bench. Jan-2025 overthinking research found 'each additional reasoning step makes things worse' once understanding is incomplete — more steps on a wrong trajectory amplify error.

keyInsights.insight: Accumulated failed attempts in the context window are themselves the cause of continued failure (context rot / context pollution). Each failed diff, error dump, and dead-end reasoning chain stays in context, competes for attention, and makes the agent MORE likely to repeat what it already tried. The corrective move is therefore counterintuitive: throw away context, don't add more to it.
keyInsights.why: This inverts the naive instinct ('explain the failure more, add more guidance'). The correct mechanism when stuck is a CLEAN RESTART that carries forward only a distilled lesson, not the transcript. This directly dictates a workflow primitive: on stall, summarize-and-reset rather than continue-and-correct.
keyInsights.evidence: Practitioner consensus: 'If you've corrected Claude's plan twice and it's still wrong, the context is cluttered with failed approaches... run /clear and write a better initial prompt' (Claude Code agent-teams guide); Cursor: 'Revert the changes, refine the plan... This is often faster than fixing an in-progress agent.' Context-rot literature: 'The agent repeats approaches it already tried' as context fills; attention dilutes as failed attempts accumulate. Sub-agents fix this by discarding working memory and returning only the distilled result.

keyInsights.insight: Altitude is anchored, not exhorted. 'Step back and think' as a bare instruction is ignored under fixation; what actually works is forcing the agent to RE-READ A FIXED EXTERNAL ARTIFACT (the acceptance criteria / original objective) and produce a structured diff between 'what the goal requires' and 'what I have actually changed so far.' The artifact must live outside the conversation so it doesn't rot.
keyInsights.why: A reassessment only triggers when there is a concrete, external thing to check against — otherwise the agent reassesses against its own polluted context and confirms its current path. The workflow needs a durable objective/acceptance-criteria artifact and a checkpoint step that re-reads it verbatim and scores current work against it, returning explicit pass/fail per criterion.
keyInsights.evidence: Workflow sources report the reflection-against-explicit-acceptance-criteria step 'catches 60-70% of issues that would otherwise surface in human review'; PIV/critic-agent patterns run a critic 'inline at the end of each phase... returning pass/fail with explanation.' Cursor: 'Agents perform best when they have a clear target to iterate against' (tests, types, linters give objective signal vs. self-assessment).

keyInsights.insight: The trigger for 'stop and reassess' must be EXTERNAL and AUTOMATIC, never left to the agent's self-judgment. A fixated agent is by definition the worst judge of whether it should stop. The trigger should be a cheap counter/hook (N repeated actions, M turns without a passing check, K self-corrections) owned by the orchestration loop — not a polite 'consider whether you're stuck' added to the prompt.
keyInsights.why: This is the crux of the brief: what makes a checkpoint trigger rather than be ignored is that the AGENT DOESN'T OWN THE TRIGGER. The loop owns it. Self-assessed stopping conditions are reliably skipped by an agent in sunk-cost mode. Bake the trigger into the harness as a hard interrupt.
keyInsights.evidence: AWS LimitToolCounts enforces hard per-tool ceilings that return 'limit reached. DO NOT CALL ANYMORE'; Latitude: 'Set hard timeout limits on session length to prevent infinite loops' and flag 'session step count anomalies.' The sunk-cost article notes humans/agents keep going 'believing the next prompt will finally work' — self-judgment is exactly what fails, so the cutoff must be external. Unambiguous SUCCESS/FAILED terminal states (not the agent's interpretation) drove the 7x improvement.

keyInsights.insight: Cheap, explicit terminal states are more powerful than clever loop-detection. The deepest root cause of looping is AMBIGUOUS FEEDBACK — the agent can't tell 'done' from 'try again,' so it retries. Designing every check to return a crisp PASS / FAIL / DONE token (not prose) prevents most loops before any detection logic is needed.
keyInsights.why: This is the highest-leverage, lowest-ceremony mechanism of all: it requires zero detection infrastructure. Make tests, builds, and checks emit unambiguous machine-readable verdicts, and structure the objective as a checklist of binary criteria. Most fixation never starts if the agent always knows exactly where it stands.
keyInsights.evidence: AWS: clear SUCCESS states alone cut 14 calls to 2 (7x) — beating both DebounceHook (12) and hard limits; 'ambiguous responses cause the agent to retry... clear states reduced tool calls.' Overthinking-via-MCP research: loops dominate cost when terminal states are unclear, requiring 'carefully designed termination conditions.' Latitude flags 'HTTP 200 with empty payloads' as a silent ambiguity trap.

antiPatterns.pattern: Continue-and-correct: when the agent goes wrong, appending another instruction ('no, do it this way', 'you forgot X') and pressing on in the same conversation.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Each correction looks locally cheaper than restarting, and the model has no backtrack primitive — so it rationalizes the existing trajectory forward. The accumulating failed attempts in context then bias it toward repeating them (context rot), making each correction less effective than the last.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Hard rule: after 2 failed self-corrections on the same objective, STOP appending. Revert to last green state and either restart a fresh session carrying only a 1-paragraph distilled lesson, or hand the scoped subtask to a sub-agent whose working memory is discarded on return. Never reach correction #3 in the same context.

antiPatterns.pattern: Self-assessed stopping ('reflect on whether you might be stuck and consider stepping back' baked into the prompt).
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: A fixated agent evaluates 'am I stuck?' against its own polluted context and confidently answers 'no, the next attempt will work' — the textbook sunk-cost rationalization. The instruction reads as satisfied without behavior changing.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Move the trigger out of the prompt and into the loop/harness: a counter (N identical-target edits, M turns with no passing check, K corrections, or a wall-clock/token budget) fires a HARD interrupt that injects the reassessment step. The agent does not get to vote on whether reassessment happens.

antiPatterns.pattern: Reassessing against the conversation instead of against the fixed objective — the agent 'reviews its progress' by reading back through its own (polluted) transcript.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The transcript is the most salient, most recent context, so the model anchors on its own prior reasoning and re-confirms the current path. Without an external invariant, 'step back' just re-derives the same plan.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Keep the objective + acceptance criteria as a durable artifact OUTSIDE the conversation (file/ticket). The checkpoint step must re-read it verbatim and emit a per-criterion PASS/FAIL table plus an explicit diff of 'goal requires' vs 'changes made so far' — not a freeform self-summary.

antiPatterns.pattern: Ambiguous, prose-y check outputs ('looks mostly good', 'there may be more issues', 'tests largely pass') that the agent must interpret.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The model treats any non-terminal phrasing as license to keep going; it cannot distinguish 'done' from 'almost', so it retries to be safe — the documented root cause of retry loops.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Every gate returns a crisp token: PASS / FAIL / DONE with the failing criterion named. Express the objective as a binary checklist. Wire tests, builds, linters, and types as the source of truth so verdicts are machine-generated, not model-narrated.

antiPatterns.pattern: Solving harder and harder sub-problems that were never required — over-engineering a fix the acceptance criteria never asked for (depth-first rabbit holes).
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Tunnel vision: the agent optimizes the local sub-goal it last latched onto, losing sight of the top-level objective; overthinking research shows extra reasoning steps on an unneeded path actively degrade outcomes.
antiPatterns.mitigation: At each checkpoint, force the question 'is this work traceable to an acceptance criterion?' Any change not mapping to a criterion is a stop-and-reassess trigger. Scope creep is a weeds signal, not just an efficiency problem.

antiPatterns.pattern: Single-trajectory commitment: the agent picks the first plausible approach and never enumerates alternatives, so a bad early choice is unrecoverable.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Greedy decoding favors the first coherent path; without an explicit 'list 2-3 approaches and pick' step the agent commits and then can only rationalize forward (the DARS local-minimum failure mode).
antiPatterns.mitigation: In the plan phase, require enumerating 2-3 distinct approaches with a one-line tradeoff each BEFORE coding (cheap, one paragraph — not parallel implementations unless the task warrants it). On a stall trigger, the reassessment step must propose a DIFFERENT approach from the enumerated list, not a variant of the failed one.

recommendations.rec: Make the orchestration loop own a STALL DETECTOR, not the agent. Track a fingerprint of each action (tool + target file/region + hash of resulting error/test output). If the same fingerprint, or an unchanged failing-check output, recurs within a sliding window of ~3 actions, fire a hard interrupt. Default thresholds: 2 repeated same-target edits, OR 3 turns with no acceptance-criterion moving from FAIL to PASS, OR 2 failed self-corrections on one objective.
recommendations.appliesTo: Harness/loop layer of every coding task; the universal first guardrail.
recommendations.keepsItLean: One counter and a fingerprint hash (~30 lines, per AWS DebounceHook). No model calls, no judge. Detects the universal symptom mechanically so you don't need per-pathology rules.

recommendations.rec: On stall-interrupt, run a fixed REASSESS step (not freeform): (1) re-read the objective + acceptance criteria from the external artifact verbatim; (2) emit a per-criterion PASS/FAIL table; (3) state in one line what was tried and why it failed; (4) propose a DIFFERENT approach from the plan's enumerated alternatives. If reassess fires twice on the same objective, escalate to revert + fresh session.
recommendations.appliesTo: The triggered checkpoint; the core 'force altitude' mechanism.
recommendations.keepsItLean: A single templated prompt fired only on trigger — zero overhead on the happy path. The fixed structure (re-read, score, diagnose, re-route) is what makes it bite instead of being a vibe.

recommendations.rec: Store objective + acceptance criteria as a DURABLE BINARY CHECKLIST outside the conversation (file or ticket). Every phase boundary re-reads it and gates on it. Express each criterion so a test/build/lint/type-check can decide it. The agent may not advance a phase until it can name which criteria are PASS and which are FAIL.
recommendations.appliesTo: Task setup + every phase transition (plan -> implement -> verify).
recommendations.keepsItLean: One artifact written once at task start; reused as the single source of truth. Replaces vague 'review your work' with a concrete invariant that can't rot in context.

recommendations.rec: Make all checks emit UNAMBIGUOUS TERMINAL TOKENS — PASS / FAIL / DONE with the failing criterion named — never prose. Wire tests, builds, linters, and type-checkers as the verdict source. This is the cheapest anti-loop mechanism: most fixation never starts when the agent always knows exactly where it stands.
recommendations.appliesTo: Every verification/tool boundary across the workflow.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Zero detection infrastructure — it's a formatting/contract change on outputs. Delivered the biggest measured win in the literature (7x) on its own.

recommendations.rec: Default to REVERT-AND-RESET over continue-and-correct. Checkpoint (commit/snapshot) at every green state. The fix-in-place budget is 2 corrections; on the 3rd, revert to last green and restart a fresh session carrying only a one-paragraph distilled lesson — or delegate the scoped subtask to a sub-agent whose exploration/failed-attempt context is discarded on return.
recommendations.appliesTo: Recovery path after stall; long or multi-file tasks prone to context rot.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Snapshots are cheap (git); the 'distilled lesson' is one paragraph, not a transcript. Discarding polluted context restores capability instead of layering more guidance onto a degraded run.

recommendations.rec: In the plan phase, require ENUMERATING 2-3 distinct approaches with a one-line tradeoff each before any code is written, and record them in the plan artifact. This gives the stall-recovery step a pre-vetted alternative to switch to instead of producing a variant of the failed path.
recommendations.appliesTo: Plan phase of non-trivial tasks (multi-file, unfamiliar code, architectural changes).
recommendations.keepsItLean: One short paragraph of upfront thinking — not parallel implementations. Cheaply installs the 'reverse gear' that greedy single-trajectory agents structurally lack (the DARS insight) without re-sampling infrastructure.

recommendations.rec: Treat OFF-OBJECTIVE WORK as a first-class stall signal. At each checkpoint, require every change to map to a named acceptance criterion; any change that doesn't (gold-plating, unrequested refactors, deepening sub-problems) triggers the reassess step. Scope creep and rabbit-holing are weeds, not just inefficiency.
recommendations.appliesTo: Every checkpoint; especially debugging and refactor tasks.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Reuses the same acceptance-criteria artifact and checkpoint already in place — one extra question ('traceable to a criterion?'), no new machinery.

sources: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2503.14269 (DARS: Dynamic Action Re-Sampling — greedy single-trajectory agents trapped in local minima, branching/backtrack mechanism)

https://dev.to/aws/how-to-prevent-ai-agent-reasoning-loops-from-wasting-tokens-2652 (AWS — DebounceHook fingerprinting, LimitToolCounts hard ceilings, clear SUCCESS/FAILED states; measured 14->2 (7x) reduction)
https://latitude.so/blog/ai-agent-failure-detection-guide (observability signals: identical repeated tool calls, session step-count outliers, hard timeouts, quality degradation past ~10 turns)

https://dev.to/samuelfaure/the-ai-programming-sunk-cost-fallacy-loop-and-how-to-break-it-13d6 (sunk-cost loop signals; hard-reset protocol; diagnostic questions on spec clarity and abstraction mismatch)
https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/context-rot-ai-coding-agents-explained (context rot: failed attempts pollute context, agent repeats tried approaches; sub-agents discard working memory)
https://cursor.com/blog/agent-best-practices (watch the diff and Stop early; revert+refine plan over fixing in place; start fresh when effectiveness drops; clear target to iterate against)
https://claudefa.st/blog/guide/agents/agent-teams-best-practices (after 2 failed corrections, /clear and re-prompt; don't patch a broken plan, re-plan from failure; /rewind to last good state)
https://arxiv.org/html/2602.14798v1 (Overthinking Loops in Agents via MCP Tools — structural loops dominate cost; need designed termination conditions)
https://arxiv.org/pdf/2510.07880 and arXiv:2508.17627 (LLM overthinking — extra reasoning steps on a wrong trajectory degrade outcomes; early-exit patterns)
Workflow/PIV + critic-agent practitioner reports (reflection against explicit acceptance criteria catches ~60-70% of issues; inline critic returns per-criterion pass/fail)

keyInsights.insight: The adjective in an ADJECTIVE-NOUN phrase is almost never a new entity. In nearly all cases it describes a STATE, VISIBILITY, ROLE, OWNERSHIP, SCOPE, or CONFIG of the existing noun. The default action when an agent encounters 'draft post / published post', 'public user / private user', 'admin user', 'active subscription / cancelled subscription', 'archived order' must be: add a field to the existing table, NOT create a new table or type. New tables are the rare exception, justified only when the adjective brings its own distinct columns, relationships, and lifecycle.
keyInsights.why: This is the single highest-leverage guardrail because the failure is silent and compounding. Two tables for 'draft post' and 'published post' means publishing requires a cross-table move (delete+insert, breaking FKs, comments, likes), every query that wants 'all posts' needs a UNION, and a third state ('scheduled') forces a third table. A status enum makes publish a one-field UPDATE and a new state a one-value addition. The wrong choice is cheap to make and brutally expensive to unwind once data and FK references accumulate.
keyInsights.evidence: Practitioner consensus models post lifecycle as a status enum ('draft','published','archived') on one table; soft-delete is a flag/timestamp on the row, not a separate 'DeletedPost' table (oneuptime Laravel soft-delete, Sudhakar's blog). The cross-table move cost is the concrete tell.

keyInsights.insight: There is a SYMMETRIC error the heuristic must also catch: over-normalizing a scalar/state/config INTO its own table or join. Modeling a single-valued status, a feature flag, a per-row setting, or a 1:1 config block as a separate entity (or as EAV key-value rows) is the opposite failure and just as damaging. An adjective that is a fixed, mutually-exclusive, single-valued property of ONE row belongs in a COLUMN on that row — not a lookup table you must join, and never an EAV (EntityID/Attribute/Value) bag.
keyInsights.why: LLMs swing both ways: they over-split nouns into tables AND over-normalize attributes into tables/EAV because both feel like 'proper' normalization. Schema complexity is not free — it directly degrades the very LLM that will later query it. The workflow's own downstream agents pay the tax.
keyInsights.evidence: EAV produces 'slow, unoptimizable SQL' and searches taking 'minutes instead of seconds'; over-normalization makes 'queries nearly impossible to read and maintain' (Medium/CodeX 10 anti-patterns; The Art of PostgreSQL). On the LLM side: 'SQL generation accuracy on simpler schemas shows higher performance compared to more complex schemas' and unclear/over-fragmented schemas are a top cause of hallucination (arxiv 2409.15907, Databrain 50K LLM SQL eval).

keyInsights.insight: The real discriminator is NOT the grammar of the phrase — it is CARDINALITY x LIFECYCLE x EXCLUSIVITY. A property that is single-valued, mutually-exclusive, and changes over the row's life => enum/status COLUMN (draft/published; active/cancelled). A property that is many-valued or assigned/revoked over time and shared across rows => a relationship / join table (a user's ROLES, a post's TAGS). A property that is a boolean capability on the row => a FLAG or permission column (is_public, can_edit). Only when the adjective carries its own attributes + relationships + independent lifecycle => a genuinely new table.
keyInsights.why: This resolves the apparent contradiction in the sources — STI guidance says 'don't make type tables' while RBAC guidance says 'roles ARE their own tables.' Both are right: 'admin user' is single-valued-ish role-as-attribute UNTIL roles become many-per-user and dynamically managed, at which point user_roles is correct. The agent needs the axis, not a blanket rule, or it will misapply one source.
keyInsights.evidence: GitLab explicitly: 'Don't design new tables using Single Table Inheritance'; warns '*_type columns are a code smell.' RBAC best practice uses Users/Roles/Permissions + User_Roles/Role_Permissions JUNCTION tables precisely because roles are many-to-many and dynamically assigned (Tutorials24x7, dev.to/peledzohar). Single-valued status => column; many-valued assignment => join table.

keyInsights.insight: Mutually-exclusive variant tables ('public_user' vs 'private_user', 'individual_account' vs 'business_account' as siblings) are the worst-performing form of over-splitting because they fracture identity and foreign keys. Every other table that references 'a user' now cannot point to one place; you need polymorphic FKs or a UNION view to ask 'all users.' The fix is one table + a discriminator column (type/status/visibility enum), with type-specific columns either nullable on the row or, if numerous, in a small 1:1 extension table keyed by the same id.
keyInsights.why: This is the adjective-noun trap at its most expensive. It is the case LLMs most confidently get wrong because the two phrases read like two nouns. Catching it preserves referential integrity for the entire rest of the schema, which the agent is about to build on top of.
keyInsights.evidence: STI/discriminator analyses: keep one table with a type column unless subclasses have 'significantly different attributes' and 'strong data integrity per type' is required, in which case class-table inheritance (shared base + 1:1 extension) — never independent sibling tables for what is one identity (Medium STI patterns; netguru Rails STI).

antiPatterns.pattern: Minting a new table or new type for each adjective: draft_posts + published_posts; public_users + private_users; admin_users; archived_orders; cancelled_subscriptions as separate entities.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The training distribution maps distinct noun phrases to distinct schema objects, and the model pattern-matches 'a published post is a kind of thing' into 'a table.' Each phrase reads self-contained, so the model never asks whether one row simply transitions between these states. It also conflates 'this concept deserves a name' with 'this concept deserves a table.'
antiPatterns.mitigation: Hard guardrail: before emitting CREATE TABLE / a new type/class/model, the agent must run the decision heuristic below. If the candidate table is the SAME real-world thing as an existing table in a different state/role/visibility/scope, it is FORBIDDEN to create a new table — add a column to the existing one. Require the agent to state, in one line, what distinct columns+relationships+lifecycle the new table has that the existing noun lacks; if it can't, it's a field.

antiPatterns.pattern: Over-normalizing a single-valued attribute into a lookup/join table or EAV (a post_status table joined to every post; a user_settings EAV with attribute/value rows for fixed per-user booleans).
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Normalization is heavily rewarded in training text as 'correct,' so the model applies it reflexively and indiscriminately, mistaking 'normalize repeating GROUPS' for 'normalize every attribute.' EAV looks maximally flexible, which the model reads as maximally good.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Rule: a property that is single-valued and mutually-exclusive per row is a COLUMN (use a CHECK/enum for the allowed values), not a joined table, and NEVER EAV. Only introduce a lookup table when the value set is large, shared, and itself carries attributes, or when the relationship is genuinely many-to-many. Ban EAV unless the attribute set is truly open/user-defined at runtime.

antiPatterns.pattern: Creating sibling variant tables for one identity (account_individual / account_business), forcing polymorphic FKs or UNION-everything views downstream.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Two well-formed noun phrases trigger two tables; the model optimizes locally for 'this variant has some unique fields' without considering that the rest of the schema must reference 'an account' uniformly. It doesn't simulate the downstream FK cost.
antiPatterns.mitigation: One identity = one base table with a type/discriminator column. Put type-specific fields as nullable columns, or in a 1:1 extension table sharing the primary key, only if they are numerous. Forbid independent sibling tables for what other tables must reference as a single foreign key.

antiPatterns.pattern: Premature tables for states/scopes that don't exist yet ('let's add a roles table / a tenants table in case'), and '*_type' columns that pre-announce future splitting.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The model generalizes toward 'enterprise-grade' schemas seen in training and adds extensibility nobody asked for, conflating thoroughness with quality.
antiPatterns.mitigation: YAGNI gate: model only states/roles/scopes that the current feature actually requires. A single role today is a column or a boolean, not a roles+user_roles+permissions+role_permissions constellation. Treat a '*_type' column as a smell that prompts the heuristic, per GitLab guidance. Escalate to relational structures only when multiplicity is real now.

recommendations.rec: Embed this as a MANDATORY pre-flight gate before any CREATE TABLE, new ORM model, new type/class, or new enum-of-entities. THE ADJECTIVE-NOUN HEURISTIC: 'Before creating <AdjectiveNoun>, is the adjective a new ENTITY, or a property OF the existing noun?' Resolve via this ordered ladder, stopping at the first match — (1) Same thing, different lifecycle stage, single-valued + mutually exclusive (draft/published, active/cancelled, open/closed) => STATUS/STATE ENUM COLUMN on the existing table. (2) A capability or boolean trait (is_public, is_verified, can_edit) => BOOLEAN/PERMISSION FLAG column. (3) Single-valued visibility/scope from a fixed set (public/private/unlisted) => VISIBILITY/SCOPE ENUM column. (4) Tied to who owns/created it (my posts vs their posts) => OWNERSHIP FK (owner_id / tenant_id), not a table. (5) A fixed per-row config/setting => CONFIG COLUMN(S) or a 1:1 settings row, never EAV. (6) Many-valued and assigned/revoked over time, shared across rows (a user's roles, a post's tags) => RELATIONSHIP / JOIN TABLE. (7) ONLY if the adjective brings its own distinct columns AND relationships AND an independent lifecycle => a NEW TABLE. Default and tie-breaker: a FIELD on the existing noun.
recommendations.appliesTo: Every schema/migration/model-creation step in the workflow; any moment the agent introduces a new persisted concept.
recommendations.keepsItLean: It is a single ordered checklist with a default of 'field, not table.' It biases toward the cheapest reversible change and only escalates to new structure on explicit evidence, eliminating whole categories of rework without adding process.

recommendations.rec: Require the agent to answer FIVE EXACT QUESTIONS in-line (one word each, in the migration comment or PR description) for any candidate new table, and to downgrade to a column unless the answers justify a table: Q1 'Is it the SAME real-world thing as an existing row, just in a different state/role/scope?' (yes => column). Q2 'Can ONE row be in two of these at once?' (no => it's a single-valued enum, not separate tables; yes => maybe a relationship). Q3 'Over its life, does a row MOVE between these values?' (yes => status column, because a table move breaks FKs/history). Q4 'Does this variant have columns or relationships the base noun does NOT?' (no => column/flag; yes-and-many => 1:1 extension or table). Q5 'How many of these will exist per parent — exactly one, or many?' (one => column; many => join table).
recommendations.appliesTo: Code-review / guardrail step and the schema-authoring step; enforce as a blocking checklist on migrations.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Five yes/no questions, answerable from the feature description alone, no external lookup. They mechanically separate the three legitimate outcomes (column / relationship / new table) and make the expensive option (new table) the one that must be argued for.

recommendations.rec: Bake in the resolution table as canonical worked examples the agent matches against, so it doesn't re-derive each time: 'draft post'/'published post'/'scheduled post' => status enum on posts (NOT N tables; publish = UPDATE status). 'public user'/'private user' => visibility enum or is_public flag on users (NOT two tables; preserves one user identity + FKs). 'admin user'/'member' => role column if single+static; user_roles join table only if roles are many-per-user and dynamically managed (the STI-vs-RBAC fork). 'active'/'cancelled'/'expired subscription' => status enum + timestamps on subscriptions. 'archived'/'deleted order' => archived_at / deleted_at timestamp (soft-delete flag), NOT an archived_orders table. 'individual'/'business account' => one accounts table + type discriminator + nullable/extension columns, NOT sibling tables. 'featured product' => is_featured boolean (+ optional featured_at), NOT a featured_products table.
recommendations.appliesTo: Schema design step; usable verbatim as few-shot examples in the agent's system/step prompt.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Concrete pattern matches are faster and far more reliable for an LLM than abstract rules; this converts the heuristic into recognition, cutting both the over-split and over-normalize errors at the source with zero added tooling.

recommendations.rec: Add a CARDINALITY-FIRST tie-breaker the agent applies whenever the heuristic is ambiguous, plus a complexity ceiling that protects downstream LLM queryability: decide single-valued-vs-multi-valued BEFORE deciding table-vs-column. Single-valued + exclusive => column (enum/flag). Multi-valued or time-assigned => relationship. Then enforce: prefer the fewest tables that preserve integrity; do not normalize a fixed scalar into its own table; never EAV for a known attribute set; treat each '*_type' column as a trigger to re-run the heuristic (per GitLab). If a chosen design would force 'all <nouns>' to become a UNION across tables, that alone is proof of over-splitting — collapse to one table + discriminator.
recommendations.appliesTo: Schema design and review steps; especially the contested role/status cases and any time the agent reaches for a second table of the 'same' noun.
recommendations.keepsItLean: One axis (cardinality) resolves most ambiguity deterministically, and the 'UNION smell' gives a single mechanical falsification test. It keeps schemas small, which the research shows directly improves the accuracy of every later LLM that has to read or query them.

keyInsights.insight: Architectural obstruction is detectable from LOCAL code-shape signals, not global judgment. The agent doesn't need to 'understand the architecture' to detect a collision — it needs a small checklist of concrete tells that fire while it is editing: about to copy-paste/duplicate logic; about to import or reach across a module/layer boundary it hasn't crossed before in this slice; a type/interface doesn't fit and it's about to widen it, cast, or add an optional field; about to add a boolean/enum flag or conditional whose only purpose is to avoid changing an existing structure; about to modify a file owned by many features (shared base class, core model, router, DI container). Each of these is a string the agent can be told to watch for in its OWN diff-in-progress.
keyInsights.why: LLMs default to the path that produces a green test fastest, which is almost always the local hack (duplicate, flag, cast). Reframing detection as 'inspect the shape of the change you are about to make' turns an abstract 'is this an architecture problem?' into a mechanical self-check the agent reliably performs.
keyInsights.evidence: Fowler/Beck 'make the change easy, then make the easy change' — the trigger for preparatory refactoring is precisely the felt friction that 'existing code structure would make adding a feature difficult' (martinfowler.com/articles/preparatory-refactoring-example.html). The signals above are the operational form of that friction.

keyInsights.insight: The single most important move is to SEPARATE the two hats in time, and make it a hard sequencing rule: a refactor commit must contain ZERO behavior change (tests unchanged, all green before and after), and a behavior commit must contain ZERO restructuring. The agent can only wear one hat per commit. This is the mechanism that prevents both failure modes at once: it stops the hack (you can't sneak a flag into a refactor commit) AND stops the derail (the feature keeps moving in its own commits).
keyInsights.why: Mixing refactor + behavior in one diff is the root cause of both unreviewable mega-PRs and silent behavior regressions. For an autonomous agent the rule is enforceable and checkable: a 'refactor' commit that changes a test assertion is a violation; a 'feature' commit that touches 15 unrelated files is a violation.
keyInsights.evidence: Two-hats originates in the earliest refactoring practice (Fowler, workflowsOfRefactoring): 'construction hat' for adding functionality, 'refactoring hat' for restructuring, 'you can only wear one hat at a time'. Fowler's preparatory-refactoring example demonstrates it: refactor with tests staying green, THEN switch hats to add the feature.

keyInsights.insight: There is a clean three-tier response, and the discriminator between tiers is BLAST RADIUS and REVERSIBILITY, not difficulty. Tier 1 (preparatory refactor, in-scope): the restructure is confined to files this slice already touches and is behavior-preserving — just do it, in a separate refactor commit, then add the feature. Tier 2 (enabling/foundation slice): a clean implementation requires building new structure or seams that outlive this feature and that future slices will also need — split it into an explicit 'enabling slice' that ships first, ideally behind a seam/facade (strangler-fig), keeping the original feature slice intact behind it. Tier 3 (escalate as architecture decision): the change forces a choice between competing designs, is hard to reverse, or touches a shared foundation many features depend on — stop coding, write the decision up, and surface it for human ratification.
keyInsights.why: Autonomous agents fail by collapsing all three into one: they either hack (treat Tier 2/3 as Tier 1) or they spiral into an unbounded refactor (treat Tier 1 as Tier 3). A blast-radius/reversibility test gives a deterministic routing rule that an agent can apply without architectural taste.
keyInsights.evidence: Maps directly to Fowler's workflow taxonomy: preparatory (Tier 1), planned refactoring as a separate scheduled task (Tier 2), and the long-lived 'transitional architecture' of strangler-fig (Tier 2 seams). 'One-way-door vs two-way-door' (Bezos/Amazon reversibility heuristic) is the standard discriminator for Tier 3 escalation.

keyInsights.insight: The enabling slice (Tier 2) should almost always be implemented as a SEAM, not a rewrite. Build the new structure behind a facade/adapter/interface, route the current slice through it, and leave the old path in place until later slices migrate to it. This is strangler-fig at feature granularity. It is what keeps the feature moving: the agent gets its clean foundation AND ships behavior in the same iteration, because the seam lets new and old coexist instead of demanding a big-bang change to the shared foundation.
keyInsights.why: The agent's instinct when blocked by a shared foundation is either to hack around it or to refactor the whole foundation (derail). The seam is the third path — it converts an unbounded foundation change into a bounded, reversible one. Critically for autonomy, a seam is reversible (delete the adapter), so it does NOT require Tier-3 escalation even though it touches foundational concerns.
keyInsights.evidence: Strangler-fig (martinfowler.com/bliki/StranglerFigApplication.html): identify 'seams where components can be isolated and replaced,' deliver incrementally while 'both systems coexist,' accept 'transitional architecture that eventually disappears.' Big-bang replacements 'go down in flames most of the time.'

keyInsights.insight: Escalation (Tier 3) must produce a tiny structured ADR-style artifact and PAUSE that decision only — not the whole build. The agent records: the obstruction, the 2-3 candidate designs with one-line tradeoffs, the reversibility classification, its recommended option, and what it will do if it gets no answer (the default/fallback). It then proceeds with the smallest reversible Tier-1 path under an explicit, tagged TODO/debt marker so the feature still ships, rather than blocking. The human ratifies async.
keyInsights.why: A purely blocking escalation derails the build (the whole point is to keep moving); a purely silent decision lets an agent unilaterally make irreversible architecture calls. The fix is 'escalate the decision, not the build': flag it loudly, take the reversible interim path, and make the debt impossible to lose.
keyInsights.evidence: Reversibility framing (one-way vs two-way doors) means most obstructions have a reversible interim that is safe to proceed on. ADR (Nygard's Architecture Decision Records) is the lean, standard artifact for exactly this — context, decision, consequences — sized to minutes, not a design doc.

keyInsights.insight: Debt and decisions must be recorded AS CODE ARTIFACTS the agent and reviewers cannot lose: a machine-greppable marker at the exact site (e.g. ARCH-DEBT(slice-id): reason + link) PLUS a one-paragraph ADR file in the repo. Prose summaries in chat or PR descriptions get dropped between iterations of a long autonomous build; an in-repo grep target and a committed ADR survive context compaction and hand-offs.
keyInsights.why: Autonomous, multi-turn builds lose context. If the 'we took a shortcut here, the real fix is X' knowledge lives only in the agent's working memory or a PR comment, the next slice (or next agent) re-derives or cements the hack. A grep-able tag is the cheapest mechanism that makes debt re-discoverable and the ADR makes the rejected alternatives recoverable.
keyInsights.evidence: Boy-scout rule ('leave it cleaner than you found it') only compounds if debt is visible; litter-pickup refactoring (Fowler) presumes you can SEE the litter. A tagged marker is the visibility mechanism. ADRs are the durable record of the chosen-vs-rejected design.

antiPatterns.pattern: Flag-driven avoidance: adding a boolean/enum/config flag or a special-case branch whose only purpose is to dodge changing an existing structure or type.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: It is the lowest-token path to a green test. The model optimizes for 'make the failing requirement pass' and a conditional is locally valid code that compiles and passes, so the reward signal fires immediately.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Make 'I am adding a flag/branch to avoid a refactor' an explicit STOP signal in the agent's self-check. When it fires, force the Tier-1/2/3 routing before writing the flag. Allow the flag only if it is a genuine feature toggle (will be read by users/ops), never as a refactor-avoidance device.

antiPatterns.pattern: Duplicate-and-diverge: copy an existing function/component, tweak the copy for the new case, leave both. The slice passes but the codebase now has two sources of truth.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Copying is safe and self-contained — it cannot break the original's tests, so it minimizes perceived risk. The model has no episodic memory that it just created a maintenance fork.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Trigger on 'about to duplicate logic'. Route to Tier-1 preparatory refactor: extract the shared core into a reusable unit (behavior-preserving, separate commit), THEN parameterize for the new case. If extraction is too large for this slice, escalate to Tier-2 enabling slice rather than duplicating.

antiPatterns.pattern: Scope explosion / refactor spiral: the agent correctly senses the architecture fights it, then 'fixes the architecture' — a 40-file refactor that derails the feature and becomes unreviewable.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Without a blast-radius bound, 'make the change easy' has no natural stopping point; each refactor reveals an adjacent thing that 'should also' be fixed, and the agent has no cost model for reviewer load or merge risk.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Hard-bound Tier-1 to files the slice already touches and to behavior-preserving edits. The moment a refactor wants to touch a file outside the slice's footprint or a shared foundation, it is no longer Tier-1: convert to a Tier-2 enabling slice (seam) or Tier-3 escalation. Cap preparatory refactors and require tests to be byte-identical before/after.

antiPatterns.pattern: Silent unilateral architecture decision: the agent picks one of several competing, hard-to-reverse designs (new data model, new cross-service dependency, new auth scheme) and just builds it, because nothing told it to stop.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The agent is rewarded for autonomy and completion; 'ask a human' reads as failure to finish. It also cannot easily tell a reversible choice from an irreversible one, so it treats all choices as equal and proceeds.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Add an explicit reversibility gate: before committing any change that introduces a new persistent schema, a new external/cross-boundary dependency, or a new public contract, classify one-way vs two-way door. One-way doors are mandatory Tier-3 escalations — write the ADR, take the reversible interim, do not cement the irreversible choice.

antiPatterns.pattern: Refactor and feature in one commit/PR, hiding behavior change inside structural churn.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The agent experiences the task as one continuous flow ('improve this then add that') and emits one diff. It has no built-in notion that reviewers diff structure and behavior separately.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Enforce the two-hats commit rule mechanically: a commit tagged 'refactor' must leave all tests unchanged and green (no assertion edits); a commit tagged 'feature' must not move code between files or rename. A diff that violates either is auto-rejected back to the agent to split.

antiPatterns.pattern: Ephemeral debt: the agent notes the shortcut only in its reasoning or the PR body, then the next slice/agent never sees it and builds on the hack.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: In-context prose feels like a sufficient record to the model in the moment, but autonomous builds compact context and hand off between iterations, so the note evaporates.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Require BOTH an in-code grep-able marker at the exact site (ARCH-DEBT(slice-id): ...) and a committed one-paragraph ADR. 'Recorded in chat/PR only' does not count as recorded.

recommendations.rec: Add a mid-implementation self-check the agent runs whenever it is about to write a change, with these exact STOP signals: (1) 'I'm about to duplicate logic that already exists,' (2) 'I'm reaching across a module/layer boundary I haven't crossed in this slice,' (3) 'The type/interface doesn't fit — I'm about to cast, widen, or add an optional field,' (4) 'I'm adding a flag/branch whose only purpose is to avoid changing existing structure,' (5) 'I'm about to edit a file shared by many features (base class, core model, router, DI/config).' If any fires, do NOT write the change — run the routing tree first.
recommendations.appliesTo: Every code-writing step inside a thin-slice build; the per-edit guardrail.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Five concrete string-match tells, checked against the diff the agent is already about to produce. No separate analysis pass, no architecture model required.

recommendations.rec: Encode the three-tier routing as a decision tree keyed on blast radius + reversibility: STEP 1 — Is the restructure behavior-preserving AND confined to files this slice already touches? YES -> Tier 1: do a preparatory refactor in a SEPARATE refactor-only commit (tests stay green and unchanged), then add the feature in a feature-only commit. STEP 2 — Does a clean implementation need new structure/seams that future slices will also use, but the change is reversible? YES -> Tier 2: insert an explicit enabling slice that ships a seam/facade/adapter first (strangler-fig); route this feature through the seam; leave the old path until later slices migrate. STEP 3 — Does it force a choice between competing designs, OR is it a one-way door (new persistent schema, new cross-boundary dependency, new public contract, shared-foundation change)? YES -> Tier 3: escalate as an architecture decision (below).
recommendations.appliesTo: The moment any STOP signal fires during a slice.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Two yes/no questions (in-footprint+behavior-preserving; reversible) deterministically route to one of three actions. No subjective 'how hard is this' judgment.

recommendations.rec: Make the two-hats rule a hard, machine-checkable commit invariant: a commit tagged 'refactor:' must not change any test assertion and must be green before and after; a commit tagged 'feat:' must not move/rename code across files. Reject any diff that mixes both back to the agent with instruction to split. State it in the system prompt as: 'You may wear only one hat per commit. Never refactor and change behavior in the same commit.'
recommendations.appliesTo: Commit/PR construction for every slice.
recommendations.keepsItLean: One invariant, checkable by a hook from the diff itself (assertion-changed? files-moved?). Replaces subjective review of 'is this PR doing too much.'

recommendations.rec: For Tier 2, default to a SEAM not a rewrite: 'When a shared foundation blocks you, do not modify the foundation in place and do not duplicate it. Introduce an adapter/facade/interface in front of it, implement the new behavior behind that seam, and route only this slice through it. Leave the existing path working.' Bound the enabling slice so it ships in the same iteration as a small standalone PR before the feature PR.
recommendations.appliesTo: Tier-2 obstructions where a shared/foundation component fights the change.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Converts an unbounded foundation refactor into a bounded, reversible adapter. The feature keeps moving; the seam is deletable, so it needs no escalation.

recommendations.rec: For Tier 3, escalate the DECISION, not the build: write a <=1-page ADR containing [obstruction] [2-3 candidate designs, one-line tradeoff each] [one-way vs two-way door] [recommended option] [reversible interim path the agent will take now]. Then PROCEED on the smallest reversible interim under a tagged debt marker, and surface the ADR for async human ratification. Do not block the whole build waiting; do not silently pick a one-way-door option.
recommendations.appliesTo: Tier-3 obstructions: competing designs or irreversible/shared-foundation changes.
recommendations.keepsItLean: A fixed 5-field ADR template, minutes to write. The build continues on a reversible path, so escalation costs throughput only on the irreversible decision, which is exactly where human input has leverage.

recommendations.rec: Record debt as code, twice, always: at the exact site insert a grep-able marker ARCH-DEBT(<slice-id>): <one line why + link to ADR>, and commit the ADR file under docs/adr/. The next slice's first step is to grep for ARCH-DEBT touching its footprint and read those ADRs before coding, so it builds on the intended design (or closes the debt via the boy-scout rule) rather than cementing the hack.
recommendations.appliesTo: Any time a Tier-2 interim or Tier-3 reversible-interim shortcut is taken; and the start of every subsequent slice.
recommendations.keepsItLean: One grep target + one short file. Survives context compaction and agent hand-offs, which prose-in-chat does not. Makes the boy-scout rule actionable because the litter is now visible and located.

recommendations.rec: Give the agent an explicit 'keep the feature moving' priority statement so it does not over-escalate: 'Tier 1 and Tier 2 are normal in-flight work — do them and continue. Only Tier 3 (competing designs or one-way doors) pauses for a human, and even then you proceed on a reversible interim. Never stop the build for an obstruction you can resolve with a behavior-preserving refactor or a reversible seam.'
recommendations.appliesTo: Global build policy; prevents the refactor-spiral and over-escalation failure modes.
recommendations.keepsItLean: A one-paragraph priority rule that resolves the tension between 'don't hack' and 'don't derail' in the agent's favor toward bounded, reversible progress.

keyInsights.insight: Guardrails must be SURFACE-TRIGGERED, not blanket. The single most important design move is a detection layer that scans the planned change for a small set of trigger signals and attaches the matching guardrail only when present. A CSS tweak, a copy change, or a pure-frontend refactor should trigger ZERO of these checks. The cost of ceremony-on-everything is that the agent (and reviewers) start ignoring the guardrails entirely.
keyInsights.why: Stripe-grade quality is about the right rigor on the load-bearing surfaces (money, mutations, migrations, trust boundaries) and deliberate lightness everywhere else. A workflow that applies idempotency ceremony to a tooltip change trains everyone to skip the checklist, which then misses the real money path.
keyInsights.evidence: Stripe's own engineering keeps the version-compat layer OUT of core business logic ('engineers only write code for the latest version and do not clutter the core business logic with version checks; the response compatibility layer takes over') — rigor is isolated to the boundary, not smeared across all code. https://stripe.com/blog/api-versioning

keyInsights.insight: Idempotency is not one check — it is a 3-part obligation that only fully applies when a mutation has a FOREIGN-STATE side effect (charge a card, send an email, call an external API). (1) Accept an idempotency key on every mutating endpoint; (2) persist all local state BEFORE the first foreign mutation; (3) pass a derived idempotency key DOWN to the external call so the downstream is also dedup'd. The deepest insight from Stripe's reference implementation: 'once we make our first foreign state mutation, we're committed one way or another' — so the recovery-point/atomic-phase structure exists precisely to make the moment-of-no-return resumable.
keyInsights.why: Most AI-generated handlers do the external call first (or interleave it with DB writes) and have no key, so a client retry double-charges or double-sends. The fix is cheap if baked in at build time and near-impossible to retrofit safely after the data is corrupted.
keyInsights.evidence: Stripe stores response_code/response_body keyed by (user_id, idempotency_key), uses recovery_point state machine (STARTED→RIDE_CREATED→CHARGE_CREATED→FINISHED), wraps local mutations in SERIALIZABLE transactions, and passes idempotency_key down to the Stripe charge call. https://brandur.org/idempotency-keys ; https://stripe.com/blog/idempotency

keyInsights.insight: Money has FOUR coupled invariants that must all hold together, not just 'use integers': (1) integer minor units (cents), never float/double; (2) currency travels WITH every amount as a non-optional field — a bare amount is a bug; (3) zero-decimal currencies exist (JPY, KRW: 1 = ¥1, not ¥0.01) so 'multiply by 100' is wrong; (4) any split/proration uses the largest-remainder method (allocate floors, then distribute leftover pennies by fractional remainder) so the parts sum EXACTLY to the whole. Float is banned not just for storage but for arithmetic.
keyInsights.why: AI models routinely emit `price * 1.0`, `parseFloat`, naked `amount` without currency, and `total / n` splits that lose or gain a penny. Each is a silent financial bug that passes tests on round numbers and fails in production on 1/3 splits and yen.
keyInsights.evidence: Stripe requires 'A positive integer in the smallest currency unit (100 cents = $1.00, or 1 to charge ¥1, a 0-decimal currency)'. Largest-remainder allocation is the payroll/dividend standard; banker's rounding avoids systematic bias when decimals are unavoidable. https://stripe.com/docs/currencies ; https://shopify.engineering/eight-tips-for-hanging-pennies ; https://www.moderntreasury.com/journal/floats-dont-work-for-storing-cents

keyInsights.insight: Safe migrations are a SEQUENCING constraint, not a code-quality one: never combine schema change + backfill + read-switch in one deploy. The expand/contract pattern forces each step to be independently backward-compatible: (1) EXPAND — add nullable column/new table, additive only, never rename/drop; (2) BACKFILL in batches with sleep, and dual-write from app code so new rows stay current; (3) CONTRACT — drop the old column only after the new path is fully deployed and verified, in a SEPARATE later migration. The guardrail an agent applies is: any migration that drops, renames, or NOT-NULLs an existing column in the same PR as the code that uses it is unsafe and must be split.
keyInsights.why: AI tends to generate the 'clean final-state' migration (rename column, add NOT NULL, drop old) in one file because it produces the tidiest diff — which is exactly the one that takes production down during the rollout window when old and new code run simultaneously.
keyInsights.evidence: Expand/contract: 'each stage is backwards compatible with the previous stage... permitting different parts of the system to be at varying release stages without synchronized deployment.' Dual-write during backfill is the critical phase. https://www.prisma.io/dataguide/types/relational/expand-and-contract-pattern ; https://blog.thepete.net/blog/2023/12/05/expand/contract-making-a-breaking-change-without-a-big-bang/

keyInsights.insight: 'Don't reinvent solved problems' has a concrete, enforceable trigger list: auth/session/password hashing, crypto/signing, payments, accounting/ledgers, dates/timezones/calendars, money math, and parsing untrusted formats (URLs, emails, CSV). When a change touches any of these, the guardrail is 'use the established library/provider, do not hand-roll.' This now carries an ADDITIONAL AI-specific risk: hallucinated package names. The agent must verify any newly-introduced dependency actually exists and is the real, popular package — not a plausible-sounding name an attacker may have squatted.
keyInsights.why: These domains have hidden complexity (DST gaps, leap seconds, timing-safe comparison, PCI scope) where a custom implementation is both more work and a latent vulnerability. For AI specifically, slopsquatting is a documented live attack: models invent package names and attackers pre-publish malware under them.
keyInsights.evidence: Documented case: AI-suggested crypto package 'didn't exist three weeks prior'; an attacker published malware under the hallucinated name and exfiltrated generated private keys. https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=24320998 ; https://medium.com/@kcl17/ai-code-is-going-to-kill-your-startup-and-youre-going-to-let-it-9f364fea242e

keyInsights.insight: Trust-boundary validation should be PARSE-once-at-the-edge, not validate-repeatedly. The guardrail fires when data crosses from untrusted to trusted (HTTP handler, queue consumer, webhook, file upload, third-party response): parse the raw input into a typed/validated domain object at the first line of the handler, reject (don't coerce) on failure, and let the type system carry the guarantee inward so downstream code never re-checks. This pairs directly with explicit typed errors: validation failure returns a structured, typed error (400 with a machine-readable code), it does not throw a naked exception or return a bare 500.
keyInsights.why: AI handlers commonly trust the request body shape, scatter ad-hoc `if (!x)` checks through business logic, and surface failures as generic 500s. Parsing at the boundary into a schema (zod/pydantic/etc.) collapses all of this into one enforceable point and makes the typed-error contract free.
keyInsights.evidence: 'Parse them once at the system's boundaries into well-typed values, and from that point on let the type system enforce correctness... If validation cannot parse the input, the request is rejected.' Sealed Result types give 'compiler enforcement — both paths required, no failure silently ignored.' https://medium.com/@trinitietp/parse-dont-validate-practical-lessons-df352da8a154 ; https://blog.codingmilitia.com/2020/03/02/aspnet-038-from-zero-to-overkill-more-explicit-error-handling-with-either/

keyInsights.insight: API/contract compatibility is a guardrail on any change to an EXISTING consumed surface (public endpoint, shared event schema, exported library function, DB view used elsewhere). The rule set, drawn from Stripe's compat policy, is concrete and checkable: ADDITIVE changes are safe (new optional param, new response field, new endpoint); BREAKING changes are not (rename/remove a field, tighten a type, make an optional param required, change semantics or default). When a change is breaking, the guardrail forces a versioning/deprecation path instead of an in-place edit. Least astonishment is the catch-all sibling: a function/endpoint must do what its name implies and nothing surprising (deleteX deletes — it doesn't soft-archive; no hidden side effects; sensible defaults).
keyInsights.why: AI freely renames fields and tightens types to make code 'cleaner,' silently breaking every existing caller because it only sees the local file, not the consumers. Encoding Stripe's additive-vs-breaking taxonomy gives the agent a bright line it can apply without org-specific knowledge.
keyInsights.evidence: Stripe's backward-compatible set: 'adding new API resources, new optional request parameters, new response properties, changing order of properties, changing length/format of opaque strings.' POLA: 'deleteFile() should remove the file, not secretly archive it'; 'method calls should never have unexpected side effects.' https://docs.stripe.com/upgrades ; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principle_of_least_astonishment

antiPatterns.pattern: Foreign-state mutation (charge, email, webhook, external POST) executed before local state is persisted, or with no idempotency key passed downstream — so a client/network retry double-charges or double-sends.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The model writes code in narrative order ('charge the card, then save the order') because that reads naturally, and it treats the external SDK call as a black box without threading an idempotency key into it. It also rarely models the retry path at all.
antiPatterns.mitigation: On any mutating handler with an external side effect: require an Idempotency-Key on the endpoint, persist local rows in a transaction BEFORE the external call, and pass a derived key (e.g. `op-{record_id}`) into the external SDK call. Store and replay the cached response on repeat keys. If the surface has no external side effect and is a single DB upsert with a natural unique key, this is overkill — skip it.

antiPatterns.pattern: Money as float/double, or a bare amount with no currency, or `amount * 100` to convert, or `total / n` to split.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Floats and `parseFloat` are the path-of-least-resistance numeric type in JS/Python; tests on round numbers ($10.00) pass; the model has no notion of zero-decimal currencies or penny-conservation in splits.
antiPatterns.mitigation: On any code touching prices, totals, balances, fees, tax, or payouts: integers in minor units only; currency is a required field next to every amount; convert using a currency-aware helper (not ×100); allocate splits with largest-remainder so parts sum to the whole. Ban `float`/`parseFloat`/`Number()` on money in review.

antiPatterns.pattern: A single migration that adds a column AND backfills AND switches reads, or renames/drops/NOT-NULLs an existing column in the same PR as the code change.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: It produces the cleanest final-state diff and matches how the model 'thinks' about the end goal; it has no mental model of the rollout window where old and new app code run concurrently against one schema.
antiPatterns.mitigation: For any migration touching an EXISTING column/table in use: split into expand (additive, nullable) → dual-write + batched backfill → contract (drop old, separate later deploy). Never rename in place (add new + backfill + drop). Brand-new tables with no readers yet are exempt — one additive migration is fine.

antiPatterns.pattern: Hand-rolling auth, password hashing, token signing, crypto, date/timezone math, or money rounding instead of using a vetted library — and/or importing a hallucinated package to do it.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The model is optimizing for a self-contained, dependency-light answer and will happily emit a custom JWT verifier or a `new Date()` timezone hack; it also confidently names packages that may not exist.
antiPatterns.mitigation: When a change touches auth/crypto/payments/accounting/dates-tz: require an established library or provider, no custom implementation. For any newly-added dependency, verify the package actually exists and is the real well-known one (check registry + download counts) before importing — guard against slopsquatting.

antiPatterns.pattern: Trusting request/payload shape and scattering ad-hoc validation through business logic; surfacing failures as generic 500s or naked thrown exceptions with no typed/structured error.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Parsing the body into a schema feels like boilerplate the model skips to get to 'the real logic'; it reaches for `throw new Error('bad input')` because that's the most common pattern in its training data.
antiPatterns.mitigation: At every trust boundary (HTTP handler, queue/webhook consumer, file upload, third-party response): parse raw input into a typed object on the first lines, reject on failure with a structured error (e.g. 400 + machine-readable code), and rely on the type downstream instead of re-checking. Map expected failures to explicit typed errors; reserve exceptions/500 for truly unexpected faults.

antiPatterns.pattern: Renaming/removing a response field, tightening a param type, or making an optional param required on an already-consumed endpoint/schema/exported function — done in place to 'clean up.'
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The model sees only the file in front of it, not the external consumers, and 'tidying' the contract looks like an improvement with no visible downside locally.
antiPatterns.mitigation: For changes to any surface other code consumes (public API, shared event, exported function, shared view): allow additive changes freely; for breaking changes (rename/remove/retype/require/semantic shift) STOP and require a versioned or deprecation path instead of an in-place edit. Apply least-astonishment: the symbol must do what its name says, with no hidden side effects and sensible defaults.

recommendations.rec: Add a SURFACE-DETECTION gate as the first step after planning. Scan the planned change for trigger signals and emit a short list of attached guardrails (often empty). Triggers → guardrail: writes to DB + external call ⇒ idempotency; identifiers/columns named price|amount|total|fee|tax|balance|currency or a payments SDK ⇒ money invariants; files in migrations/ touching an existing column ⇒ expand/contract; imports/usage of auth|crypto|jwt|date-tz|payments ⇒ use-a-library + verify-package; HTTP handler / webhook / queue consumer / file upload ⇒ boundary parse + typed errors; edit to a public route, exported symbol, or shared event schema ⇒ compat taxonomy. If no trigger matches, print 'no Stripe-grade guardrails apply' and proceed.
recommendations.appliesTo: Every build task, as a cheap pre-implementation routing step.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Most tasks (CSS, copy, internal refactors, new isolated code) match zero triggers and pay nothing. Rigor is spent only on detected load-bearing surfaces.

recommendations.rec: Encode the idempotency guardrail as a 3-line obligation the agent must satisfy ONLY when a mutation has a foreign side effect: (1) endpoint accepts Idempotency-Key; (2) local DB writes committed before the external call, in a transaction; (3) a derived key is passed into the external SDK call and the response is cached for replay. If the mutation is a single idempotent upsert on a natural unique key, mark it 'naturally idempotent' and skip.
recommendations.appliesTo: Mutating endpoints/jobs that charge, send, provision, or call external services.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Skips entirely for reads, pure-local upserts, and anything with a natural unique constraint. No state-machine ceremony unless there are 2+ foreign mutations to sequence.

recommendations.rec: Bake money invariants into a single review assertion: 'amounts are integer minor units; currency is present beside every amount; no float/parseFloat on money; conversions use a currency-aware helper (zero-decimal aware); any split uses largest-remainder so parts sum to the whole.' Fail the change if any clause is violated. Provide a one-line allocation helper in the prompt so the agent has a correct split to copy.
recommendations.appliesTo: Any code computing or storing prices, totals, fees, tax, payouts, balances, or proration.
recommendations.keepsItLean: It is one checklist applied only to money-touching diffs; non-financial numeric code (timeouts, counts, pixels) is untouched.

recommendations.rec: Make the migration guardrail a hard split rule: 'A migration that drops, renames, or adds NOT NULL to an existing in-use column, OR that backfills in the same step as a read-switch, must be split into expand → backfill+dual-write → contract across separate deploys. Renames become add-new + backfill + drop-old.' New tables with no readers are exempt (single additive migration allowed).
recommendations.appliesTo: Schema changes to tables/columns already in production use.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Greenfield tables and additive nullable columns pass in one step with no extra phases — the multi-phase dance only triggers on genuinely destructive/breaking changes.

recommendations.rec: Combine 'don't reinvent' with AI-specific dependency safety into one step: when the change touches auth, crypto, payments, accounting, or dates/timezones, require an established library/provider (no hand-rolled implementation) AND verify every newly-added package actually exists and is the genuine popular package (registry + download-count check) before importing, to defeat slopsquatting.
recommendations.appliesTo: Changes importing or implementing security-, money-, or time-sensitive functionality, and any change adding a new dependency.
recommendations.keepsItLean: The library-mandate fires only on the named high-risk domains; the package-existence check is a quick lookup run only when a NEW dependency is introduced, not on every edit.

recommendations.rec: Pair boundary-validation and typed-errors into one guardrail: at each trust boundary, parse untrusted input into a typed/schema-validated object on the handler's first lines, reject (don't coerce) with a structured error carrying a machine-readable code and correct status (400/401/403/409/422), and reserve exceptions/500 for unexpected faults only. Downstream code consumes the typed value without re-validating.
recommendations.appliesTo: HTTP handlers, webhook receivers, queue/event consumers, file/CSV uploads, and parsing of third-party responses.
recommendations.keepsItLean: One parse site per boundary replaces scattered checks, so it reduces code rather than adding it. Internal function-to-function calls inside the trust boundary are exempt.

recommendations.rec: Apply the compat + least-astonishment guardrail only when editing a surface other code consumes: allow additive changes (new optional param, new field, new endpoint/function) freely; for breaking changes (rename/remove a field, tighten a type, require a previously-optional param, change semantics/defaults) require a versioned or deprecation path instead of an in-place edit; and assert the symbol does what its name implies with no hidden side effects and sensible defaults.
recommendations.appliesTo: Public/consumed APIs, exported library functions, shared event/queue schemas, and DB views used by other services.
recommendations.keepsItLean: Private, single-caller, or brand-new code is exempt and can be changed freely — the taxonomy only gates surfaces with real external consumers.

keyInsights.insight: A slice is THIN when it does ONE observable behavior, and COMPLETE when the app is runnable and that behavior is real after the slice merges. Both conditions must hold simultaneously. 'Thin' is about scope of behavior; 'complete' is about depth through the stack. The failure mode is sacrificing one for the other: thin-but-incomplete (a schema with no endpoint that uses it) or complete-but-fat (the whole feature in one slice).
keyInsights.why: This is the precise definition the workflow must encode. Most slicing guidance is vague ('small stories'). The operational test is binary and checkable: (1) can you name the single user-observable change in one sentence without 'and'? (2) if you stopped right after this slice, is the app still runnable and is that one behavior actually exercisable by a user or caller? If either answer is no, it is not a valid slice.
keyInsights.evidence: Humanizing Work: a vertical slice means 'when complete, the system is observably more valuable to a user'; horizontal/task splits 'fail the independent and valuable criteria.' Shape Up's done-criteria: Integrated + Clickable + Validating (works end-to-end with live data).

keyInsights.insight: Sequence work most-unknown-to-least-worrisome and integrate end-to-end in the FIRST slice — not most-foundational-first. The instinct to build the 'base layer' first is exactly backwards: it maximizes the time before you learn whether the design holds.
keyInsights.why: This directly contradicts the default LLM behavior of 'let's set up the schema/models/scaffolding first.' The workflow should force the first slice to pierce every layer the feature touches (UI/API -> logic -> storage) for the single thinnest path, even if everything else is stubbed. This de-risks the architecture on day one and guarantees a runnable app throughout.
keyInsights.evidence: Shape Up Ch.10: pick the first slice by Core + Small + Novel; 'teams build one meaningful piece of work end-to-end early on and then repeat'; finishing 80% of design and 60% of backend separately is an 'illusion of progress' with 'nothing demonstrable.' Stripe/Shopify incrementalism: 'diff by diff, endpoint by endpoint' parity — never a big-bang integration.

keyInsights.insight: There is a reliable priority order for HOW to slice, derived from Lawrence's nine patterns. Slice by (1) workflow steps — build the simplest happy path end-to-end, defer middle steps; (2) CRUD operations — one operation, not 'manage'; (3) business-rule / data variations — handle one case, defer the rest; (4) data-entry method — plainest input first; with 'defer performance' and 'spike' as last resorts. This ordering systematically produces thin vertical slices and starves horizontal ones.
keyInsights.why: Gives the workflow a concrete generative procedure rather than 'use judgment.' When an agent faces a feature, it should walk this list top-down and take the first pattern that yields a runnable end-to-end increment. Crucially, 'make it work before make it fast' and 'spike only as last resort' prevent two classic time sinks.
keyInsights.evidence: Humanizing Work's nine patterns in priority order; SPIDR (Spike, Path/workflow, Interface, Data, Rules) is the same idea compressed. Meta-pattern: 'identify the core complexity, list the variations, reduce all variations to one complete slice.'

keyInsights.insight: Two splits are equally thin; pick the one that (a) lets you DELETE or deprioritize a slice, and (b) yields roughly equal-sized slices. A good split exposes low-value work you can now choose not to do.
keyInsights.why: This is the highest-leverage tie-breaker and it doubles as a value filter. If splitting reveals that 60% of the imagined feature is a rare variation nobody asked for, the slice boundary just saved that work. The workflow should ask after each proposed split: 'which of these resulting slices could we ship without and still deliver the core value?' — then sequence those last or drop them.
keyInsights.evidence: Humanizing Work evaluation rules: choose splits that 'let you deprioritize or throw away a story' and that 'produce more equally-sized stories.' Shape Up's scope-hammering separates must-haves from nice-to-haves so nice-to-haves can be cut under the fixed appetite.

keyInsights.insight: A foundation/enabling slice is justified ONLY when it is consumed by the very next slice and reduces total risk; otherwise it is fake work. The honest test: a foundation slice must be paired with — and immediately followed by — a behavior slice that exercises it, OR it is a time-boxed spike whose output is knowledge (a decision), not committed production scaffolding.
keyInsights.why: LLMs love to generate plausible scaffolding (full schemas, base classes, config systems, abstraction layers) that no current slice uses. The guardrail: no infrastructure is allowed to merge unless a user-observable behavior in the same or next slice uses it. 'Build infrastructure once, then add variations' (Lawrence's Major Effort pattern) is legitimate, but only after the first variation has proven the infrastructure is needed.
keyInsights.evidence: Humanizing Work: 'Avoid implementing infrastructure without user-facing functionality'; Major Effort pattern allows building infra once but only in service of imminent variations. Shape Up: spikes/uphill work resolve unknowns; the deliverable is reduced uncertainty. SPIDR's Spike is explicitly research-only and time-boxed. Hex/Stripe Strangler Fig: new infra is grown at the edges behind real traffic, never ahead of it.

keyInsights.insight: Boundaries stay honest when the slice is sized to be 'demoable in days, not weeks' and the demo is a real user action, not a passing unit test. 'Clickable with live data' is the boundary-integrity check.
keyInsights.why: The cheapest way to keep slices both thin and complete is to require a demo of the observable behavior as the definition of done. If you can't demo it because 'the UI isn't wired up yet' or 'it needs the other endpoint,' the boundary was dishonest — it was a horizontal slice wearing a vertical costume. The workflow's verification step should be: trigger the one behavior through the real entry point.
keyInsights.evidence: Shape Up: first slice 'tangible and demoable early — in the first week or so'; done = Integrated + Clickable + Validating. INVEST 'Small' rule of thumb: 6-10 per sprint. GSD: 'keep each cycle small, observable, and shippable' via tracer bullets that go end-to-end first.

antiPatterns.pattern: Horizontal-first sequencing: 'Let's design the full schema / build all the models / scaffold all endpoints, then wire up behavior.' Producing a data layer, then a service layer, then UI as separate steps.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Layered/clean-architecture training data presents code organized by layer, so the model reproduces that as a build ORDER. It also feels efficient to 'do all the DB work at once.' And a complete-looking schema is easy to generate and looks like progress.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Guardrail: the FIRST slice must touch every layer the feature uses for exactly one thinnest path; all other paths are stubbed/hardcoded. Forbid any slice whose deliverable is a single layer (schema-only, endpoints-only, types-only). Ask: 'name the one user-observable behavior this slice delivers' — if the answer is 'none yet, this is groundwork,' reject or convert to a time-boxed spike.

antiPatterns.pattern: Fat slice disguised as one feature: bundling create+read+update+delete, happy path + all error cases + all variations, into one 'implement X' chunk because they're conceptually related.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: The model optimizes for a complete, polished artifact in one pass and treats 'manage X' as atomic. 'And' in the task description gets flattened into one implementation.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Apply the splitting priority order (workflow step -> single CRUD op -> single variation -> simple input). Trigger words 'manage', 'and', 'all', 'support various' must force a split. Ship ONE operation/one variation end-to-end first; queue the rest as follow-on slices that each keep the app runnable.

antiPatterns.pattern: Speculative foundation: building config systems, base classes, generic abstractions, or full schemas 'we'll need later' that no current slice consumes.
antiPatterns.whyLLMsDoIt: Anticipating future needs reads as senior engineering in training data; the model front-loads flexibility to avoid being asked to refactor. Abstractions are cheap to generate and feel safe.
antiPatterns.mitigation: Rule: no infrastructure merges unless a behavior in the same or immediately-next slice exercises it. Build infra at its first real point of use, then generalize on the SECOND use (rule of two). Convert genuine unknowns into a time-boxed spike whose output is a written decision, not committed scaffolding.

antiPatterns.pattern: Incomplete thin slice: a slice that's small but leaves the app broken or the behavior unreachable — e.g., adds an endpoint with no caller, a migration with no code path, a function behind a flag that's never flipped.
